data.title: Iran agrees to suspend nuclear program, Trump says - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump said Friday that Iran has agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely and will not receive any frozen funds from the U.S. as part of a potential agreement to end the war in the Middle East.

In a phone interview with Bloomberg, Trump said a deal to end the conflict is mostly complete. The president said talks over a lasting deal will “probably” be held over the weekend. “Most of the main points are finalized. It’ll go pretty quickly,” the president said.

Iran has not yet commented on any deal beyond the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, nor on claims made by Trump on Thursday that Tehran had offered concessions, including over the key issue of its nuclear program.

Trump said he has not decided who would lead a U.S. delegation for talks with Iranian officials to sign a deal, Bloomberg reported. When asked if he would travel to Pakistan, which hosted the first round of negotiations, the president said, “I may.”

Vice President JD Vance led discussions with Iranian officials last weekend. Trump said he was considering sending Vance alongside his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and envoy Steve Witkoff for the upcoming talks.

The president denied that the moratorium on Iran’s nuclear program would expire after 20 years. When asked if the program will completely halt, Trump responded, “No years, unlimited.”

data.title: Fed's policy will depend on length of Middle East conflict, San Francisco Fed's Daly
data.description:
Uncertainty continues to loom over a solid U.S. economy, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly said Friday. That makes it difficult to provide a sense of what the Federal Reserve will do next in interest rates, she added.

"The fundamentals of the economy are quite solid," she said at the University of California, Berkeley's Fisher Center for Real Estate & Urban Economics' Spring 2026 Policy Advisory Board meeting.

The Fed's rate path will depend largely on how long the Middle East conflict lasts. If it ends quickly and oil prices go down, then "we're back on that path for interest rates where we were before," and there's no lasting damage to that path.

What she worries about the most is that persistently higher oil prices will “chip away” at consumers' ability to buy other things.

Daly describes the current federal funds rate target range of 3.50%-3.75% as "slightly restrictive."

Monetary policy is currently in a "very good place," she said. "Right now, it's not constraining the economy so much that the labor economy is faltering, not letting go of the reins completely so that inflation has no bridle."

The central bank can leave policy where it is, which would still restrain inflation, for the year. If inflation took off, which is not her modal outlook, then the Fed could raise. If  the labor market falters or the conflict ends soon, then it could adjust down.

Another factor that may eventually factor into rate decisions is productivity growth and artificial intelligence.

Productivity is rising. "That's a good thing for the economy." Daly said.

Domestic labor growth is near zero. Combined with almost no immigration growth, that means the U.S.'s labor force growth is near zero. Many businesses that rely on immigrants for their labor force are adopting more labor-saving technology, she said.

Regarding AI, "no technology has ever reduced employment, on net, ever," Daly said. "It changes who gets employed and what skills get employed."

Productivity may help with disinflationary pressure.

data.title: DeepSeek enters talks to raise $300M to better compete with frontier AI labs: report
data.description:
DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) has initiated its first fundraising campaign in hopes of raising $300M for a valuation of more than $10B, according to The Information.

The China-based company intends to enhance its funding to better take on AI rivals such as OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), the report said. The additional funds could help DeepSeek hire more AI experts and build more capable models.

DeepSeek rocked the tech sector last year when it claimed to develop a model nearly as powerful as ChatGPT or Claude but with only a fraction of the computing costs. 

However, its market share has rapidly declined over the past 12 months. In April 2025, DeepSeek held 10% of the daily average user market share among chatbots, according to a tracker by Apptopia. However, that had declined to 3.3% by March 2026.

data.title: IBM appears set up to top first quarter estimates as it builds on AI narrative: analysts
data.description:
IBM (IBM) appears poised to surpass consensus estimates when it releases its first quarter 2026 financial results post-market on Wednesday, April 22, as it builds on its AI strategy, according to analysts.

"While the prior year ended with a broad-based beat in Q4, the software outperformance came despite slower than expected growth from Hybrid Cloud, aka Red Hat," said RBC Capital Markets analysts, led by Matthew Swanson, in an investor note. "We'd expect things to pick up where they left off with a top-line beat driven by infrastructure and software strength being driven by Automation and Transaction Processing."

Although Red Hat growth slowed to 8% from 12% in constant currency during the fourth quarter of 2025, IBM expects double-digit growth in 2026.

RBC maintained its Outperform rating but reduced its price target to $330 from $361.

Wedbush also retained its Outperform rating and $330 price target ahead of earnings.

"Recent announcements continue to reinforce IBM's AI narrative heading into the print," said Wedbush analysts, led by Dan Ives, in a Friday investor note. "On April 1, IBM stated that 11 AI and automation software solutions received FedRAMP authorization, with the solutions deployed on AWS GovCloud to support secure and compliant government use cases, expanding IBM's footprint in federal and regulated markets. IBM also recently announced new cybersecurity measures to help enterprises confront agentic attacks, including a cybersecurity assessment and IBM Autonomous Security, a multi-agent-powered service designed to deliver coordinated response and intelligence at machine speed, further broadening IBM's AI value proposition into security."

During the call, RBC analysts expect IBM to address concerns related to Anthropic's (ANTHRO) COBOL modernization solution. They also expect to hear more about the Confluent acquisition as well as quantum computing initiatives.

A consensus estimate expects IBM to report adjusted EPS of $1.81 and GAAP EPS of $1.37. The revenue estimate is $15.64B. During the same quarter in 2025, IBM reported adjusted EPS of $1.60 on revenue of $14.54B.

data.title: Dow, LyondellBasell top S&P 500 losers as oil's plunge pressures chemical industry pricing, margins
data.description:
Dow (DOW) and LyondellBasell (LYB) are Friday's two largest losers on the S&P 500, down 11.6% and 11.4% respectively, as the apparent reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and signs of progress in talks between the U.S. and Iran drive a sharp pullback in energy and petrochemical prices.

Among other decliners in the sector: Celanese (CE) down 8.4%, Methanex (MEOH) down 8.2%, Olin (OLN) down 6.8%, Tronox (TROX) down 4.6%, Chemours (CC) down 4%, Huntsman (HUN) down 3.8%.

The potential easing of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East is pressuring chemical equities because lower oil and gas prices typically compress spreads and reduce pricing power across key products such as polyethylene and other derivatives.

The companies had rallied in recent weeks on the back of higher crude prices, constrained global supply, and optimism around improving industrial demand, but with those tailwinds now fading and feedstock costs normalizing, investors are resetting expectations.

data.title: Crypto stocks jump on Strait of Hormuz news, stablecoins unaffected
data.description:
While the U.S. stock market opened in the green on Friday on the back of Iran declaring the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) rallied to ~$77.77K during morning trading.

Cryptocurrencies and related stocks were pushed higher on the news. However, stablecoins were largely unaffected.

BTC-USD is currently trading at its highest level since February this year. U.S. spot Bitcoin ETFs saw net inflows of $26.05M yesterday, led by iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF's (IBIT) $81.71M inflow. Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF (BTC) recorded inflows of $16.67M and Morgan Stanley Bitcoin Trust ETF (MSBT) of $13.36M, data from SoSovalue showed.

Other cryptocurrencies were also trading higher on Friday. Ethereum (ETH-USD) was up 4.03% to $2,445.2, Solana (SOL-USD) was 1.18% higher to $90.11, and Ripple (XRP-USD) was 3.26% up to $1.50. 

Crypto stocks impacted by the news include:

  • Strategy (MSTR) +12.80% to $168.01
  • Robinhood Markets (HOOD) +6.67% to $92.64
  • Riot Platforms (RIOT) +6.50% to $18.02
  • Coinbase Global (COIN) +6.24% to $212.29
  • Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) +5.08% to $23.58
  • CleanSpark (CLSK) +5.08% to $12.00
  • Hut 8 (HUT) +5.06% to $74.51
  • Bullish (BLSH0 +4.39% to $43.24

Meanwhile, top stablecoins, including Tether (USDT-USD), USDC (USDC-USD), PayPal USD (PYUSD-USD), Ripple (RLUSD-USD), and USDD (USDD-USD), were unaffected by the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Progress on a related regulation has stalled as Senator Thom Tillis is said to have delayed the release of the CLARITY Act stablecoin yield draft, which was expected this week.

Tillis is reportedly waiting for a date when the Banking Committee will review or amend the bill.

Circle Internet Group was 3.13% lower to $104.10.

data.title: Top ranked oil stocks amid crude price plunge as Iran opens Hormuz
data.description:
Crude oil prices plunged sharply on Friday as Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared the Strait of Hormuz "completely open" for all commercial vessels for the remaining duration of the ceasefire, the latest sign that a diplomatic resolution to the U.S.-Iran conflict may be within reach.

The United States Oil Fund (USO) fell over 10% on Friday morning, reflecting the broader sell-off in crude futures. This continues a broader downtrend that began with the initial two-week ceasefire.

Oil prices, while still significantly elevated versus pre-conflict levels, are steadily giving back the war premium built up over the past six weeks.

Against this backdrop of falling crude prices, below is a list of the top 10 oil stocks ranked according to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings. The list includes companies from various segments of the energy sector, including integrated oil and gas, refining and marketing, exploration and production, and equipment and services.

The list is topped by Exxon Mobil (XOM), with a near-perfect Quant Rating of 4.97 and a market cap of $631.71B. PBF Energy (PBF) and Par Pacific Holdings (PARR) follow closely behind, with Kodiak Gas Services (KGS) and Chevron Corporation (CVX) rounding out the top five. The list features a diverse range of market capitalizations, from energy giants like Exxon Mobil and Chevron to smaller players like National Energy Services Reunited Corp. (NESR) with a market cap of $2.37B.

Here is the list:

  1. Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM), Quant Rating: 4.97

  2. PBF Energy Inc. (PBF), Quant Rating: 4.96

  3. Par Pacific Holdings, Inc. (PARR), Quant Rating: 4.93

  4. Kodiak Gas Services, Inc. (KGS), Quant Rating: 4.93

  5. Chevron Corporation (CVX), Quant Rating: 4.92

  6. Valero Energy Corporation (VLO), Quant Rating: 4.91

  7. SM Energy Company (SM), Quant Rating: 4.90

  8. National Energy Services Reunited Corp. (NESR), Quant Rating: 4.89

  9. Crescent Energy Company (CRGY), Quant Rating: 4.87

  10. Murphy Oil Corporation (MUR), Quant Rating: 4.85

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: Simmons signals high end of 9%-11% 2026 NII growth while targeting ~10.5% CET1
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Simmons First National Corporation (SFNC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Jay Brogdon tied the quarter’s loan momentum to multi-year internal changes, saying, “we've been focused on quality growth for really a few years now,” citing “changes in behaviors, changes in incentive plans, changes in how we target clients that we want to grow,” while also noting “very, very robust demand” alongside “uncertainty in the macro” and “pricing competition.”
  • Brogdon described commercial strategy as shifting toward deeper relationships and fee capabilities: “We've been really, really investing heavily in commercial treasury management, really our full commercial payment suite of products,” and said the bank is pursuing “total banking relationship focus.”
  • Brogdon highlighted early traction from a newly hired wealth management team: “that group has already brought over about... over $350 million in AUM.”
  • CFO Daniel Hobbs described margin drivers as balance-sheet and funding actions, saying, “we came in 3 basis points linked quarter growth,” while emphasizing “a focus on driving our funding and deposit costs lower through remixing of the balance sheet,” including that “we reduced time deposits, kind of grew our core deposit base.”

Outlook

  • CFO Hobbs reiterated the 2026 framework and rate-sensitivity context, stating, “our guide was NII of 9% to 11% growth,” and adding, “I said NIM would probably be in the mid-3.80s by the end of the year.” He updated the rate-cut assumption backdrop versus the prior guide: “recall that the guide had 2 rate cuts... As you look at the forward today, there are 0 rate cuts.”
  • Hobbs signaled a bias within the existing NII range: “as we move forward to the end of the year, we're probably going to be looking at the high end of that range of that 9% to 11% range,” while stressing deposit capacity as a limiter: “I think the biggest governor of our loan growth is going to be how we're able to grow deposits.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Hobbs quantified mix and pricing dynamics: “Loan yields were down 7 basis points,” and on funding, “on the deposit cost side, we're down 48 basis points.” He also framed cumulative sensitivity: “loan betas are only down a little bit less than 15% and the cumulative interest-bearing deposit beta is down 63 basis points.”
  • Hobbs provided CD repricing detail: “the CDs that matured was in the 3.56% range and what went on was in the 3.13% range,” adding, “there's probably 1 to 2 more quarters' worth of CD repricing benefit there.”
  • On deposit composition and trends, Hobbs said, “Consumer makes up about 47%,” and added, “we're growing NIB and IB consumer deposits in that 2% to 3% range,” while noting, “we've got some work remaining to do on the commercial NIB side.”
  • On valuation items, Hobbs stated, “the net of all of it was... negative $1.8 million,” and added, “just the one item, a little over $2 million.”

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: asked what drove “10% annualized” loan growth and sustainability; CEO Brogdon answered, “I don't want to promise 10% annualized loan growth every quarter,” while pointing to “quality growth” programs and cautioning about “uncertainty in the macro” and “pricing competition.”
  • Feaster, Raymond James: asked about leadership hires and wealth team progress; CEO Brogdon said new leaders are “8 or 9 weeks” in, emphasized relationship-deepening, and said the wealth team has “over $350 million in AUM” transferring or committed.
  • Woody Lay, KBW: asked about NIM amid deposit competition; CFO Hobbs said, “we came in 3 basis points linked quarter growth,” reiterated “NII of 9% to 11% growth,” and said the bank is “probably... looking at the high end of that range,” while emphasizing deposit growth constraints.
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about operating leverage and credit/charge-off comfort after new nonaccruals; CEO Brogdon said, “I'm confident in the plus side of that 5% outlook,” and on credit added, “we significantly alleviated that in the first few days in April,” and “we feel confident in our net charge-off outlook.” CFO Hobbs clarified, “The $30 million is not just one loan.”
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked about demand, CRE pull-through, CD pricing, and buybacks; CEO Brogdon said CRE pull-through was limited by “discipline from a pricing point of view,” citing “bigger banks... more aggressive,” and added, “We are still seeing a pretty elevated paydown environment.” CFO Hobbs provided CD repricing ranges, and Brogdon said on buybacks, “we continue to exercise patience,” while also noting, “we can buy back stock at a pretty low PE multiple right now on forward earnings.”
  • Brian Wilczynski, Morgan Stanley: asked about capital proposals and NPL drivers; CFO Hobbs said proposals “will be beneficial for us,” with “The LTV component... very helpful,” and CEO Brogdon reiterated, “our definition of optimal or most efficient capital is in and around kind of 10.5% CET1.” On NPLs, Brogdon said the relationship is “construction of some relatively large 1 to 4 family properties,” and CFO Hobbs quantified, “a little over $18 million.”
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked about deposit spot rate and SBIC valuation; CFO Hobbs said, “for the month of March, it was $195 million,” and on valuation stated, “negative $1.8 million.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive tone, with emphasis on momentum and durability, including Feaster calling it “a terrific quarter for growth,” while also probing sustainability, deposit competition, and credit migration.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared Q&A framing, with repeated confidence language around guidance bands and strategy, including Hobbs saying the bank is “probably... looking at the high end of that range,” and Brogdon emphasizing discipline while noting “uncertainty in the macro.”
  • Versus last quarter, management maintained a similar posture on funding discipline and organic growth, but in Q1 more explicitly tied outcomes to talent additions and indicated a stronger lean within the existing NII range.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s growth narrative shifted from Q4’s “low to mid-single-digit growth” loan-growth guide language (CEO Jay Brogdon) to Q1’s emphasis that recent results “clearly demonstrates the capabilities,” while still cautioning, “I don't want to promise 10% annualized loan growth every quarter.”
  • The margin and NII framing stayed anchored to the same 2026 guide, but Q1 updated rate-cut assumptions and leaned toward range positioning, with CFO Hobbs saying the forward now implies “0 rate cuts” and the bank is “probably... looking at the high end” of the “9% to 11%” NII growth range.
  • Analyst focus in Q1 concentrated on sustainability of outsized growth, deposit mix/remix, and specific credit migration details, while Q4 questions leaned more into pipeline composition, longer-run NIM range framing, and post-repositioning normalization.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Brogdon cited “uncertainty in the macro” and “pricing competition,” and later said the bank saw “a pickup in competition from bigger banks coming into some of the CRE products.”
  • CFO Hobbs flagged funding sensitivity, saying, “the biggest driver of what that NIM could be is the deposit side,” and added that loan growth is governed by deposit growth.
  • On credit, Brogdon characterized migration as “very isolated or episodic” and said, “there's nothing sort of new or surprising to us,” while acknowledging nonaccrual/NPL movement tied to “a legal proceeding.”

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1’s loan growth and margin progress reflected multi-year changes in incentives, client targeting, and talent, while keeping discipline around pricing amid heightened competition. The company maintained its 2026 outlook framework, with the CFO pointing to “0 rate cuts” in the current forward view versus prior assumptions and indicating the bank is “probably... looking at the high end” of its “9% to 11%” NII growth range, with NIM aimed for the “mid-3.80s” by year-end. Capital priorities remained led by organic investment and the dividend record, while management reiterated an “in and around... 10.5% CET1” view of optimal capital and continued to weigh buybacks with “patience.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Truist targets $5B of 2026 buybacks while projecting 2%-3% net interest income growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered net income available to common shareholders of $1.4 billion or $1.09 per diluted share for the first quarter,” said William Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO), adding that the quarter included “strong noninterest income growth led by our investment banking and wealth management businesses” and “250 basis points of year-over-year positive operating leverage.”

  • Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO) tied a key earnings driver to Project Finance, saying “a meaningful portion of this benefit is realized through reductions to our tax provision rather than reported revenue,” and that this “contributed to our lower tax provision in the first quarter and is a factor in our expected lower tax rate for 2026 compared to 2025.”

  • Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO) introduced a higher long-term profitability framework, stating, “we’re establishing a long-term ROTCE target of 16% to 18%,” while also saying, “we remain firmly on track” for the prior “full year 2027 ROTCE target of 15%.”

  • Michael Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) summarized the quarter’s headline results: “We reported first quarter 2026 GAAP net income available to common shareholders of $1.4 billion or $1.09 per diluted share,” and noted “revenue decreased 1.9% linked quarter due to lower net interest income primarily related to day count.”

Outlook

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) revised 2026 net interest income expectations: “For full year 2026, we now expect net interest income to increase 2% to 3% compared with our prior expectation of 3% to 4% growth,” attributing the change to “our expectation that the federal funds rate will remain unchanged throughout 2026 compared with our previous expectation for two 25 basis point reductions.”

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) lifted fee-growth expectations: “We now expect high single-digit growth in noninterest income compared with our prior expectation of mid- to high single-digit growth,” while also updating expenses: “we now expect full year GAAP noninterest expense to increase approximately 1.75% in 2026 versus our previous expectation of 1.25% to 2.25% growth.”

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) said capital return assumptions increased: “we’re now targeting $5 billion of share repurchases in ’26 versus our previous expectation of $4 billion,” and added, “our overall earnings expectations for 2026 remain unchanged.”

  • For Q2, Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) guided: “we expect revenue to remain relatively stable relative to the first quarter revenue of $5.2 billion,” “net interest income to increase approximately 1%,” “noninterest income to decline approximately 1% linked quarter,” and “Noninterest expense of $3 billion in the first quarter is expected to increase by 3% to 4% linked quarter.”

Financial Results

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) reported balance sheet trends: “Average loans held for investment increased $2.3 billion or 0.7% on a linked quarter basis to $327 billion,” while “Average deposits increased 0.7% linked quarter.”

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) detailed funding costs and margin: “Average interest-bearing deposit costs declined 14 basis points linked quarter to 2.09%,” “average total deposit costs declined 9 basis points to 1.55%,” and “Our net interest margin decreased by 5 basis points linked quarter to 3.02%.”

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) highlighted fee momentum, stating “Investment Banking and Trading income increased $37 million or 11% linked quarter to $372 million,” and that noninterest income increased “11.6% versus the first quarter of 2025.”

  • On credit and tax, Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) said, “Our effective tax rate in the first quarter was 12.4%,” and described asset quality as steady: “Net charge-offs increased 4 basis points linked quarter to 61 basis points,” while the indirect auto NPL increase was “primarily due to a change in the nonaccrual criteria.”

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division asked about NII guidance and deposit competition; Michael Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) replied that removing expected cuts mattered because Truist is “positioned liability sensitive on the short end,” and added, “it is competitive,” citing “more rotation on product” and “more yield seeking,” while pointing to “great momentum on the fee side.”

  • Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division asked about the higher buyback plan; Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) said, “I wouldn’t attribute the increase from $4 billion to $5 billion to the Basel III proposal,” framing it as “a follow-through on how we’ve been thinking about capital management.”

  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous LLP asked about the new 16% to 18% target; Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) said, “I see our ROA profile moving closer to, call it, [ 1.20% ] in that sort of ’27 outlook,” while Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO) cited “accelerating our growth in fees,” “expanded NIM,” and “efficiencies in our business.”

  • L. Erika Penala, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division asked about M&A speculation; Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO) said the article “has been refuted,” and reiterated, “we’ve set forth a plan that achieves mid-teens EPS growth over an extended period of time.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on NII sensitivity, deposits, and strategic alternatives, including Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division pressing “what’s the temperature on the degree of competition?” and asking under what conditions Truist would consider “another merger of equals” or “selling to another bank.”

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more firm in Q&A, with repeated emphasis on execution and targets, including Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO) stating, “we’re focused, we’re aligned, and we’re executing well,” and reiterating on M&A, “that’s just not a priority for us.”

  • Versus last quarter, management provided more explicit longer-term return framing (a new “16% to 18%” target), while analysts shifted from probing how to reach 15% ROTCE to challenging whether the franchise should be monetized via M&A and how sustainable fee and deposit momentum is.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted on rates and NII: in Q4, Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) forecast “two 25 basis point reductions,” while in Q1 he revised to “the federal funds rate will remain unchanged throughout 2026,” lowering NII growth to “2% to 3%.”

  • Capital return stepped up: in Q4, Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) said, “we’re targeting approximately $4 billion of share repurchases,” while in Q1 he said “we’re now targeting $5 billion.”

  • Strategic messaging extended: in Q4, Rogers (Executive Chairman & CEO) declined to “speculate” beyond 15% ROTCE; in Q1, he announced “a long-term ROTCE target of 16% to 18%,” linking it to “expected changes to the capital framework” and operating efficiency themes such as AI.

Risks and Concerns

  • Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) flagged deposit and rate sensitivity pressures behind the NII revision, citing competitive dynamics and product rotation, and said the NII guide assumes “the continued benefit from fixed rate asset repricing.”

  • On portfolio scrutiny, Maguire (Senior EVP & CFO) addressed NDFI exposure: “loans classified as NDFI represented 12% of total loans,” while “private credit… represent about 1% of our loan portfolio,” describing “advance rate limits, borrowing base mechanics and meaningful equity positions.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a quarter of stronger underlying momentum and higher long-term return ambition, anchored by $1.4 billion of net income, a new 16% to 18% long-term ROTCE target, and an increased 2026 share repurchase plan to $5 billion, while revising 2026 net interest income growth down to 2% to 3% due to a “higher for longer” rate outlook and pointing to accelerating fee businesses and a lower expected 2026 tax rate as offsets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
